sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=20, col=30), ACTION7, MOUSE(row=20, col=30).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 51, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Cross positions shifted after cross 1 removal. Current crosses at (20,30), (20,36), (26,42), (38,42). The removal sequence was: activate cross -> ACTION7 -> click transformed square. Let me try this for the cross at (20, 30).
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 50 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 1.6x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The sequence activate -> ACTION7 -> click transformed did NOT remove the cross. Still 4 green crosses at the same positions. But looking at the image, I can see:
- Cross at (20, 30) has a dark gray frame (active)
- The square at (20, 30) has a gray cross pattern (transformed)
- There's also a gray cross pattern at (20, 36) - the square where cross 3 is
So the sequence activated the cross and transformed its square, but clicking the transformed square didn't remove the cross. It just deactivated the cross (since clicking a different cross resets).
Wait, but I clicked the same cross's position. Let me re-examine.
Actually, looking at the image more carefully:
- The cross at col 30 has a dark gray frame (active)
- The square at col 30 has a gray cross pattern (transformed)
- The square at col 36 also has a gray cross pattern
So the cross is still active (dark gray frame), and the square is transformed. But clicking the transformed square didn't remove the cross. Maybe I need to click a different part of the square, or maybe the sequence is different.
Wait, let me re-read the image. The cross at (20, 30) has a dark gray frame. The square at (20, 30) has a gray cross pattern. The square at (20, 36) also has a gray cross pattern.
So ACTION7 transformed both the active cross's square AND another square. Interesting.
Actually, looking at the image, I see:
- Col 30: dark gray frame around the square, gray cross pattern inside
- Col 36: gray cross pattern inside (but no dark frame)
- Col 42 (bottom): green crosses
So ACTION7 transformed the active cross's square (col 30) and also transformed the square at col 36 (where cross 3 is).
This is different from what happened with cross 1. Let me think about why.
Actually, I think I misremembered what happened with cross 1. Let me check the history.
Looking back at the earlier turns, when I activated cross 1 and used ACTION7, it transformed cross 1's square. Then clicking the transformed square removed cross 1.
But now, when I activate cross at (20, 30) and use ACTION7, it transforms both the active cross's square AND the square at col 36. And clicking the transformed square doesn't remove the cross.
The difference might be that cross 1 was the first cross (leftmost), while the current cross is not.
Or maybe the game state is different because cross 1 was already removed.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe I need to:
1. Activate cross at (20, 36)
2. Use ACTION7
3. Click the transformed square
Let me try this.
Actually, wait. Looking at the image, the cross at (20, 30) is still active (dark gray frame). And there are transformed squares at col 30 and col 36. So the current state is:
- Cross at (20, 30) is active
- Squares at col 30 and col 36 are transformed
- 4 green crosses remain
Let me try clicking the square at col 36 (where cross 3 is) to see if that does something.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model: Activate -> ACTION7 -> click didn't remove the cross. Cross at (20,30) is active, squares at col 30 and 36 are transformed. Let me try clicking the transformed square at col 36 (where cross 3 is).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click the transformed square at col 36 (where cross 3 is)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 20, 'col': 36}], intent='solve')
print(f"Click col 36: gameplay={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}, done={last_action_result.get('done')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
dark_gray = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'G']
green = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"Dark gray: {len(dark_gray)}, Green: {len(green)}")
print(f"level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")

for n in green:
    b = n['boundary']
    center_r = sum(p[0] for p in b) / len(b)
    center_c = sum(p[1] for p in b) / len(b)
    print(f"  Green at ({center_r:.0f}, {center_c:.0f})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Click col 36: gameplay=True, done=False
Dark gray: 1, Green: 4
level_completed=False
  Green at (20, 30)
  Green at (20, 36)
  Green at (26, 42)
  Green at (38, 42)
